title: The Best Brands for Short Men Under 5'8" (And What to Actually Buy From Each) | StyleScore
heading: The Best Brands for Short Men Under 5'8" (And What to Actually Buy From Each)
description: Looking for brands that actually fit if you're under 5'8"? This guide breaks down the best brands for short men, what to buy from each, and the fit details that make the real difference.
cardDescription: Looking for brands that actually fit if you're under 5'8"?
ctaHeadline: See which proportion issue is making you look shorter than you are.
ctaBody: Take the free StyleScore style quiz and see how your short men style choices stack up across fit, shoes, grooming, wardrobe, color coordination, and occasion dressing.
content:
You grab a pair of chinos from a standard retailer, try them on, and the break pools around your ankle like you borrowed them from a taller cousin. The waist fits. The seat fits. But the length makes you look like you're wading through fabric.

This is the specific problem that most style guides skip when they talk about the **best brands for short men under 5'8"**. It's not about finding "slim" fits or "modern" cuts. It's about finding brands that engineer their clothes around a shorter torso, shorter inseam, and compressed proportions — not as an afterthought, but as the starting point.

And here's what nobody says out loud: most men don't want to spend their weekend cross-referencing fit charts and measuring tape readings. You want a short list of brands that work, what to buy from each, and why it matters for your frame. That's this.

---

## Why Standard Sizing Works Against You If You're Under 5'8"

Most menswear is designed around a 6'0" fit model. That's not a conspiracy — it's how pattern grading works. When a brand grades down to a size Small or a 30" waist, they shrink the garment proportionally, but they rarely shorten the rise, sleeve pitch, or jacket length at the same rate.

The result: a size Small shirt with a collar that sits half an inch too low, a jacket hem that hits at the wrong point on your hip, or trousers with a rise so long it visually compresses your torso. [GQ has noted](https://www.gq.com/story/how-to-dress-if-youre-short) that shorter men often struggle not with finding clothes that fit the body, but clothes that fit the *proportions* — and those are two different problems.

The brands below solve the proportions problem, not just the size problem.

---

## The Shortlist

These aren't ranked by prestige or price. They're ranked by how reliably they solve the fit problems shorter men run into every single time they shop.

### 1. ASOS (With the Right Filter)

ASSOS gets dismissed as fast fashion, which is fair in some product categories and unfair in others. Their **ASOS Design** range includes a dedicated "Short" length on most trousers and jeans, with a genuine 28" inseam option — not just a hemmed-down standard pair, but a proportionally adjusted cut with a shorter rise built in.

What to buy: The ASOS Design Slim Chinos in Short length (~$40–$55). The fit through the thigh is narrow enough to read as intentional without restricting movement.

What to skip: Their blazers in standard sizing still run long in the body. If you're buying a blazer here, stay in the dedicated "Short" suit jacket range.

### 2. Banana Republic (Factory and Mainline)

Banana Republic's **Slim and Athletic fits** are cut with a noticeably shorter rise than their heritage cuts, which immediately helps men under 5'8" avoid the dropped-crotch problem that plagues standard trousers. Their Traveler Chino in a 28" or 29" inseam hits cleanly just above the shoe on most men at 5'6"–5'7", which means less tailoring.

The mainline gets expensive fast. Banana Republic Factory runs 40–50% off almost constantly, and a pair of Factory slim trousers at $45 is genuinely solid everyday wear — not a consolation prize.

What to buy: The Slim Rapid Movement Chino. The fabric holds a crease without looking stiff, and the tapered leg keeps the ankle from reading as baggy.

### 3. Uniqlo

Uniqlo is probably the single most reliable brand for shorter men at any price point. Full stop.

Their **Smart Ankle Pants** are designed to hit above the ankle by default — not because they're cropped, but because the inseam is cut at roughly 26"–27", which lands naturally on most men between 5'5" and 5'7" without any tailoring at all. [Esquire has called Uniqlo](https://www.esquire.com/style/mens-fashion/a35826/uniqlo-review/) one of the most consistent sources of well-proportioned basics in menswear, and for shorter men specifically, their Slim Fit Oxford shirts have a shorter body length that doesn't untuck constantly or billow over the waistband.

What to buy: Smart Ankle Pants ($39.90), the Slim Fit Oxford Shirt, and the Merino Crew Neck Sweater. Those three pieces work as a complete outfit formula — slim ankle trousers, tucked Oxford, sweater layered over. Clean, proportional, done in under a minute of thinking.

### 4. Buck Mason

Buck Mason gets less attention than it deserves in conversations about **brands that fit short guys**. Their tees and henleys are cut with a body length of roughly 27"–28" from collar to hem — versus the 29"–30" you get from most brands — which means they don't bunch at the waist when tucked or drape past the hip when worn out.

Their Curved Hem Tee is specifically designed to be worn untucked without looking like a tent. At 5'7", that hem hits at exactly the right point on the hip to extend the visual leg line rather than cutting across it.

What to buy: The Curved Hem Tee ($38) and the Brushed Pima Henley. Both are worth the price because the proportions are right out of the bag.

### 5. Todd Snyder

If you're willing to spend more, Todd Snyder's **Slim and Sutton fits** are consistently cut shorter in the jacket body than most American tailoring brands. The Sutton Suit Jacket in a 36S or 38S hits at the right hip point for men at 5'6"–5'8", meaning the jacket doesn't swallow your frame or make your legs look truncated.

Here's where conventional advice gets it half right: shorter men are told to always buy Short sizes in suits. The size label matters less than the actual jacket length measurement. Todd Snyder's Short sizes are *actually* short — not just a slightly narrower chest with the same body length. [Permanent Style has written](https://www.permanentstyle.com/2021/09/how-to-dress-shorter-men.html) that jacket length is the single most critical proportion call for shorter men in tailoring, and Snyder consistently nails it.

What to buy: The Sutton Suit in a Short size if you're between 5'6" and 5'8". Pair it with their flat-front Slim Trousers and the suit reads as deliberate rather than borrowed.

### 6. Bonobos

Bonobos built their brand on solving fit problems, and for shorter men, their **Stretch Slim Chino** in a 28" or 29" inseam is one of the cleanest off-the-shelf trouser options available. The front rise is shorter than most competitors at the same inseam, which keeps the trousers from compressing your torso or making your legs look abbreviated.

Their online fit quiz is more granular than most — it asks about height, build, and how you want the fit to feel, which gets you closer to the right option without a guessing game.

What to buy: Stretch Slim Chino in a 28" or 29" inseam (~$98 full price, frequently discounted). In navy and khaki, these two pairs cover most business-casual situations without overthinking it.

### 7. Indochino (Made-to-Measure)

For suits specifically, Indochino's made-to-measure service starts around $399 and lets you specify exact measurements — jacket length, trouser inseam, sleeve length — down to the half inch.

For shorter men, that means setting a jacket body length of 28"–29" instead of the standard 30"–31", which keeps the hem from dropping below the hip and shortening your legs visually. You can also specify "no break" or "slight break" on the trouser hem directly, rather than hoping the standard length works out.

This isn't the brand for everyday basics. But for a suit you'll wear to interviews, weddings, or presentations, having those measurements locked in is worth the price — and cheaper than buying off the rack and paying a tailor to reconstruct the thing.

---

## Three Outfit Formulas That Work Across These Brands

Brands matter less than the combinations you build with them. Here are three formulas that work specifically for men under 5'8".

**Formula 1 — Smart Casual:**
Uniqlo Smart Ankle Pants + Buck Mason Curved Hem Tee (untucked) + clean white sneakers. The ankle-length trouser and hip-length tee create one continuous vertical line. Nothing interrupts it.

**Formula 2 — Business Casual:**
Bonobos Stretch Slim Chino (28" inseam) + Banana Republic Slim OCBD (tucked) + leather loafer. The tucked shirt keeps the waistband visible, which maintains the visual break between torso and leg — a proportion trick that actually holds up. See our guide on [how clothes should fit if you're shorter](/blog/how-clothes-should-fit-short-men) for more on why that waistband line matters.

**Formula 3 — Layered:**
Uniqlo Merino Crew Neck + ASOS Short Slim Chino + Chelsea boot with a modest heel. If you're adding an outer layer, keep it fitted and short — a slim zip-up or structured bomber rather than an oversized flannel. Volume on top shortens you visually. Our [layering guide for short men](/blog/layering-guide-short-men) goes deep on this, but the short version is: keep the outer layer close to the body and cropped at the hip.

---

## What Brand Descriptions Won't Tell You

Brand fit guides are useful but written to sell clothes, not to hand you the specific numbers that matter. Here's what to actually check.

**Jacket length:** For men at 5'6"–5'8", a jacket body of 28"–29" keeps the hem at the hip. Anything longer starts to visually shorten your legs.

**Trouser rise:** A front rise of 10"–11" works for most shorter men. High rise can work if the trouser is slim through the leg — a high-rise wide-leg trouser is going to look like a lot of fabric on a shorter frame.

**Shirt body length:** If a shirt measures more than 29" from collar seam to hem, wearing it untucked is going to be a problem. Buck Mason and Uniqlo both consistently come in under that mark.

**Sleeve length:** Standard shirts run 34"–35" sleeves. If you're under 5'8" with proportional arms, you're likely a 32"–33" sleeve. This is where most off-the-rack shirts fail shorter men — the sleeve extends past the wrist and bunches at the cuff. The [Esquire guide on measuring yourself for clothes](https://www.esquire.com/style/mens-fashion/a26638254/how-to-measure-yourself-for-clothes/) walks through exactly how to get this number right in under five minutes.

If you want a fast read on how your current wardrobe stacks up against these numbers, the [StyleScore assessment](https://stylescore.live) runs through your fit details and flags the specific gaps. Takes about three minutes.

---

## How to Shop These Brands Without Wasting Time

Knowing the brands is one thing. Shopping them efficiently is different — and most men are not looking to turn this into a hobby.

**Filter by inseam first, not waist.** On ASOS, Bonobos, and Banana Republic, filtering to a 28" or 29" inseam immediately eliminates 80% of the options that won't work. Start there and work backward.

**Use actual measurements, not guesses.** Measure your chest, waist, and inseam with a tape measure. It's worth doing once so you stop ordering the wrong size and returning things.

**Order two inseam lengths when you're unsure.** ASOS and Bonobos both have easy returns. If you're between a 28" and 29" inseam, order both and return the one that doesn't work. Faster than deliberating.

**Read the measurements, not the label.** Not all brands define "Short" the same way. Some shorten the chest and leave the body length unchanged. Check the actual product measurements in the description before buying.

---

## The Fit Calls That Matter More Than the Brand

Here's something that runs counter to most menswear content: the brand matters less than the specific fit within that brand. A well-proportioned shirt from H&M will look sharper on your frame than a badly proportioned one from a premium retailer. The label is not the point.

The fit calls that consistently work for men under 5'8":

- **Trousers that break at or just above the shoe.** Not a full break, not a pool. A slight break at most. When in doubt, go shorter — our [inseam guide](/blog/inseam-guide-short-men) covers the specifics, but the rule holds almost universally.
- **Jackets that end at the hip bone.** If you can feel the hem hitting below your hip when you stand straight, the jacket is too long.
- **Shirts that don't extend past the seat of your trousers when untucked.** If the hem hangs below your back pockets, it's too long to wear untucked cleanly.
- **Shoes with a low-profile toe box.** A chunky square toe cuts the leg line. A sleeker, slightly elongated toe keeps the line moving.

You're not going to run through this checklist every time you get dressed. You don't need to. You need these calls to become instinctive — and they do, once you've bought a few things that actually fit and can feel the difference in the mirror.

---

## A Note on Price and Priorities

You don't need to spend a lot. You need to spend in the right order.

A $40 pair of Uniqlo ankle trousers that fits perfectly will always look sharper than a $150 pair of standard chinos that need $60 of tailoring to fix the length and rise. The math is obvious once you see it.

If you're building from scratch, prioritize trousers and outerwear first. Those are the pieces where proportion problems are most visible and most expensive to fix after the fact. Shirts and knitwear are easier to find in proportional fits across a wider range of brands and prices.

For outerwear specifically, coat length is the single biggest proportion call you'll make in that category. A coat that hits mid-thigh on a 6'1" man might land just above the knee on a 5'7" man — and that changes the entire silhouette.

The goal isn't a perfect wardrobe. It's a wardrobe where nothing is actively working against you.

If you want the personal version of this instead of the generic advice, take the [StyleScore style quiz](/style-quiz) and see which category is actually holding your look back.

faq.question: Which clothing brands make clothes specifically for short men under 5'8"?
faq.answer: Uniqlo, ASOS (Short range), Bonobos, and Buck Mason consistently produce well-proportioned clothes for men under 5'8" without requiring significant tailoring. For suits, Todd Snyder and Indochino are the strongest options at their respective price points.

faq.question: What inseam length should a man under 5'8" look for in trousers?
faq.answer: Most men between 5'5" and 5'7" will find a 28"–29" inseam hits cleanly at or just above the shoe with little to no tailoring needed. Men closer to 5'8" may prefer a 29"–30" inseam depending on shoe height.

faq.question: Is it worth paying for made-to-measure clothing if you're a short man?
faq.answer: For suits and dress trousers, yes — particularly if off-the-rack sizing consistently misses your rise or jacket length. Indochino starts around $399 and lets you set exact measurements for both, which solves the two biggest tailoring problems shorter men face.

faq.question: What's the most common fit mistake short men make when shopping off the rack?
faq.answer: Buying standard-length trousers and assuming a tailor will fix the hem. The real problem is usually the rise — a long rise compresses the torso visually, and hemming the leg doesn't touch that.

faq.question: Can short men wear oversized or relaxed-fit clothing?
faq.answer: One relaxed piece works if everything else is fitted — an oversized tee with slim ankle trousers, for example. Stacking multiple relaxed-fit pieces adds visual bulk and shortens the perceived leg line.

faq.question: Where's the best place to shop online for short men's clothing?
faq.answer: ASOS and Bonobos are the most practical starting points — both offer dedicated short inseam options, clear measurements, and easy returns. Uniqlo is reliable for basics. For made-to-measure suits, Indochino is the most accessible mid-range option.
